does: This paper develops a mechanistic interpretability framework for understanding MLP layer neurons in fine-tuned language models by modeling them as agents in hedonic coalition games. The core method extracts stable neuron coalitions—groups whose joint ablation produces non-additive effects—using top-responsive utilities and the PAC-Top-Cover algorithm. Applied to LLaMA, Mistral, and Pythia models fine-tuned for information retrieval reranking tasks, the approach identifies coalitions with synergistic contributions and tracks their transformations across layers (persistence, splitting, merging, disappearance). The framework reveals that task-specific features concentrate in mid-layer MLPs as structured coalitional groups rather than isolated neurons, providing a more granular view of how these layers encode learned representations.
connects: This work extends prior mechanistic interpretability research that has studied individual neurons and their roles within transformers, building on the observation that neurons operate synergistically rather than independently. It responds to earlier probing studies suggesting that statistical priors shift across depth, and complements findings about LoRA updates concentrating new features in specific MLP layers. The paper bridges transformer circuit analysis—which has examined interpretable components in attention heads—with recent work on MLP layer structure. By importing coalitional game theory from economics and multiagent systems, it provides a novel formal framework for addressing the challenge of understanding how neurons collectively encode task-specific information in high-dimensional MLP spaces.
enables: This interpretability framework enables more targeted editing and control of fine-tuned language models by identifying which neuron coalitions drive specific behaviors or predictions. It opens new directions for understanding feature learning during fine-tuning and could support safer model editing by revealing how task-specific coalitions form and interact. The approach potentially allows researchers to predict how architectural or training modifications affect coalition structure, facilitating the design of more interpretable or controllable models. Future work could extend coalitional analysis to attention mechanisms, apply it to understanding failure modes or adversarial vulnerabilities, or use coalition stability as a metric for evaluating model robustness across distribution shifts.
